Q1: Honestly, my study routine was pretty scattered. I'd try to set aside time, maybe 30 hours a week on paper, but it rarely felt organized. I'm more of an auditory learner, so I'd listen to lecture recordings while doing other stuff, but I wouldn't always sit down with a proper plan. Some weeks I'd cram everything into a couple of days, other weeks I'd barely touch anything until something was due.
Q2: My pattern definitely varied based on deadlines and what else was going on. I'm involved in extracurriculars, so some weeks those took priority and studying got pushed aside. When exams came around, I'd suddenly be studying way more intensely, but during regular weeks it was pretty inconsistent. I never really locked into a set schedule that I stuck to.
Q3: Usually what got me to log in was either a deadline coming up or someone in my peer group mentioning something about an assignment. Sometimes I'd get a notification and think, okay, I should probably check what's happening. It wasn't really a habit—more like a reaction to external pressure. If nothing was immediately due, I could go days without logging in.
Q4: There were brief periods where logging in felt more automatic, especially when we had group discussions going on and my peers were active. The peer support thing actually helped because people would tag me or ask questions, so I'd feel like I should show up. But that routine never really stuck long-term—it would fade once the group activity died down.
Q5: The video lectures were probably the most useful for me since I learn better by listening. I'd play them in the background and actually absorb more than when I tried reading. The discussion posts were okay too because hearing other people's perspectives helped clarify things I didn't get. Those felt more engaging than just staring at PDFs.
Q6: I pretty much ignored the reading materials and a lot of the optional resources. PDFs and long text documents just don't work for me—I zone out after a few paragraphs. I'd tell myself I'd get to them later but rarely did. Some of the supplementary stuff felt like busywork, so I prioritized what was graded.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if I could see how it connected to passing the course or if it was actually interesting. If someone explained why something mattered, I was more likely to engage. But if it just felt like checking a box with no clear purpose, I'd skip it or rush through it. Relevance and clarity made the difference.
Q8: Yeah, optional activities felt way different. If something was required, I'd at least attempt it, even if I didn't put my best effort in. Optional stuff though? I'd look at it and think, why bother if it doesn't count? It's not that I didn't care about learning, but when you're juggling a lot, optional things get cut first.
Q9: Definitely. I'd download lecture recordings and listen to them on my phone while commuting or doing chores. Sometimes I'd study with friends in person, talking through concepts without any of us being logged into anything. I also used YouTube videos to understand topics the course materials didn't explain well. A lot of my actual learning happened away from the LMS.
Q10: There were times I'd be discussing course material with my peer support group in person or over WhatsApp, really working through problems together. None of that showed up anywhere. Or I'd replay a downloaded lecture multiple times while walking around—the LMS would only show I accessed it once. My engagement looked way lower than it actually was.
Q11: Yeah, all the time. I'd download a video or save some notes, then never go back to the LMS for that material. I'd study from those files for days, but the system would only register that one initial access. It was just more convenient to have stuff on my phone than to keep logging in repeatedly.
Q12: Around midterms, things got really hard. I had extracurricular commitments piling up and coursework from other classes. I felt stretched thin and stressed out. My attendance dropped because I was trying to manage everything and something had to give. It wasn't that I stopped caring—I just ran out of bandwidth.
Q13: Absolutely. When I was tired or overwhelmed, I'd log into the LMS but just kind of stare at it without really doing anything productive. Competing responsibilities from my activities and other courses meant the LMS often got the leftover scraps of my attention. Confusion about assignments also made me avoid them until the last minute.
Q14: Oh yeah, plenty of times. I'd open a video and let it play while scrolling my phone, technically watching but not really absorbing anything. Or I'd click through readings just to mark them as done. I was present but not really there mentally. It felt like going through the motions.
Q15: I postponed assignments constantly, even when I knew they were important. Part of it was feeling overwhelmed—the pile seemed so big that starting felt pointless. Sometimes I'd convince myself I worked better under pressure, which was only half true. Looking back, I think I was avoiding the stress of actually confronting how behind I was.
Q16: Deadlines basically controlled everything. I'd be pretty inactive until a deadline loomed, then suddenly I'd be logging in constantly, watching videos, scrambling to submit something. It wasn't healthy, but that pressure was the main thing that got me moving. Without deadlines, I probably would have engaged even less.
Q17: Feedback had a mixed effect. When I got good feedback, I felt motivated for a bit, like maybe I could actually do this. But if feedback was critical or came too late, I'd feel discouraged and pull back. The peer support helped because getting encouragement from classmates felt less intimidating than formal instructor feedback.
Q18: The course structure was pretty flexible, which sounds good but honestly made it easier for me to drift. If things had been more rigid with clear weekly checkpoints, I might have stayed on track better. The open-endedness let me procrastinate without immediate consequences until everything piled up at once.
Q19: The discussion forums were designed in a way that made engagement easier because I could just respond to peers casually. But the assignment structure was confusing sometimes—I wasn't always sure what was expected, which made me avoid starting them. Clearer instructions would have helped a lot.
Q20: I think that happens when someone is trying to figure things out but struggling to actually produce work. Like, you're watching videos and reading discussions because you want to understand, but when it comes to assignments, something blocks you—confusion, anxiety, perfectionism. Activity doesn't always translate to output.
Q21: That kind of describes me in some ways. My LMS activity looked low, but I was doing stuff offline and with peers that didn't get tracked. Plus, I'm decent at exams when I actually focus, so I could pull off a passing grade without my online activity reflecting my actual preparation. The system doesn't see everything.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns like when you log in and what you click on, but they miss the quality of engagement. They can't tell if you're actually thinking deeply or just passively scrolling. They also miss all the offline studying, peer conversations, and the mental effort that doesn't involve clicking anything.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about what you're learning and putting mental effort into understanding it. It's not just showing up or clicking through materials—it's being curious, asking questions, connecting ideas. For me, real engagement happened in conversations with peers more than in solo LMS activities.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how much I learned from my peer support group or the conversations we had outside the platform. It wouldn't capture the times I was genuinely interested but just didn't log in to show it. And it definitely wouldn't show my stress levels or how my extracurriculars affected my ability to engage consistently.
